brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses
The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving
Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong
fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually
either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities
LinkedIn profile: Johnny Sheng
Based in Arcadia, California, United States
LinkedIn About (verbatim): ai @ Photon | arts tech biz @ Iovine and Young Academy

Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged

The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

generations.story:
THE GOAL:
Close 14 deals in LA was the proof-of-concept — now the team wants to replicate that machine in new cities. "The goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent]. To do that without burning the founder out, they need a pipeline that doesn't depend on someone physically driving streets or manually cross-referencing county CSVs.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now — "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. And the raw input they do have — a ~10k-row county CSV — is poisoned: "our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. You can't build a city-expansion playbook on a foundation where nearly one in three contact records is a dead end. Every wrong address or bad email is a door knocked on the wrong house, a mailer that bounced, a conversation that never happened.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Driving for dollars and manual BeenVerified-style lookups worked when the team was learning one market [manual_research]. But that approach doesn't scale to a second city — it scales to a second hire, then a third, then a manager to manage the hires. They already see it: "either we need to hire someone else on the team or I could get like an assistant" [expansion_intent]. The $15k vendor quote they walked away from — "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal] — confirms they know the manual path has a ceiling. Paying $15k for 40 enriched records at 30%-wrong data quality is not a system; it's an expensive guess.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the patty — takes the raw 10k-row county CSV and turns it into a grounded story for each record: who actually owns it, what their situation is, and which ones carry a real forced-sale signal (tax delinquency, absentee mailing address, distress proxy from build year + no remodel). Every claim traced to a real record, not a scraper guess. The name + LinkedIn Google-search hack hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong problem [data_pain]. The output is a durable CRM-style data system — rerunnable as the team enters each new city — not a one-time enrichment drop that needs to be rebuilt in two months. Pricing is structured the same way it worked for another real-estate investor client: flat base + commission, "anywhere from 4-5K would be sick" [seller_pack], which is a fraction of the $15k quote they already refused [vendor_refusal] and gets them a living system instead of 40 enriched rows.

Opening line (seller's voice): "You've got 10,000 rows sitting there and a pipeline at zero — I can tell you which 200 of those are actually in a forced-sale window before you spend a dollar on outreach or a day driving a new city."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team enters city two with a pre-sorted, story-grounded lead list — not a raw CSV and a prayer. The 30%-wrong contact rate drops because every record is verified before the outreach dollar is spent [data_pain / seller_pack]. The founder isn't hiring an assistant to do manual lookups; the system runs the lookups. And because the data layer is rerunnable [seller_pack], city three costs less to spin up than city two — the machine compounds instead of requiring a new hire at every new market. They stay in the powerful position: choosing which deals to pursue, not scrambling to find any deal at all.

generations.story:
THE GOAL:
Scale the off-market acquisitions machine beyond LA — find distressed owners faster, in more cities, without adding headcount. In the founder's own framing: "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. "We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The team has a raw county data CSV but no CRM, no enrichment layer, and no reliable way to know which rows are worth calling. Their own email data is "consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain] — meaning roughly one in three outreach attempts misfires before it even starts.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Driving for dollars and manual title lookups worked at LA scale, but they hit the ceiling: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" [manual_research]. When the team looked at buying their way out of this with a vendor, the quote came back at "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched" — so they walked: "this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. Manual stays broken; the $15k vendor doesn't fix the 30%-wrong problem and doesn't scale to new cities. The only option they've named is hiring an assistant [expansion_intent] — but a hire doesn't rebuild the data layer, it just adds another person doing the same broken lookups.

THE BETTER PATH:
This is exactly the problem a durable enrichment system solves — not a one-time scrape that goes stale, but a rerunnable, CRM-style data layer that turns a raw county CSV into grounded stories: which owner is likely distressed, why, and what signal says so [seller_pack]. The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method clears ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. LinkedIn outreach on clean, enriched data runs ~40% reply rate vs. ~2% on cold email [seller_pack] — that's the difference between a pipeline and a dead list. Pricing is structured as flat base + commission, anchored well below the $15k vendor the team already refused [seller_pack, vendor_refusal].

Opening line, in the seller's voice: "You're sitting on a county CSV that's probably 30% wrong — before you hire anyone or touch another city, let's find out which rows actually have a forced-sale story behind them."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team goes into the next city with a pre-sorted, story-backed lead list — not a spreadsheet full of bad emails and cold guesses. The 30%-wrong data problem gets cut before it burns outreach budget. The founder can evaluate new markets from a desk instead of a windshield, and the system reruns as the CSV grows — no rebuild every two months, no $15k vendor, no new hire just to do manual lookups in a different zip code. That's the position to plan from.

THE GOAL:
This team's mandate is simple and ambitious at once: stop working just LA and start replicating the off-market acquisition playbook in new cities. As the founder put it, "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent]. That means building a system that scales — not one that requires a person in a car.

THE OBSTACLE:
Right now there is no pipeline. "We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The team just completed a major cycle and the cupboard is bare. To refill it, they fall back on what they know: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" [manual_research]. Driving works in one zip code. It does not work in Phoenix or Dallas. And even when the manual research produces a name, the contact data is broken — "our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. A 30% error rate on cold outreach means roughly one in three conversations never starts.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The team already looked for a shortcut and got burned by the price. A vendor quoted "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. That refusal was rational — $15k for 40 records is not a system, it's a line item. But "stick doing it manually" is not a city-expansion strategy either. Manual driving-for-dollars does not port to a new market. A raw county data file sitting on a laptop, with no enrichment layer and no way to sort who is actually distressed versus who just has an old address, produces the same 30%-wrong dead ends at scale. The old way is not failing slowly — it already produced an empty pipeline at the exact moment the team wants to grow.

THE BETTER PATH:
What this team needs is not a vendor and not a hire — it is a durable data layer that turns a raw county pull into a ranked, story-grounded list of who is actually likely to sell and why, with every claim traced to a real record so the human making the pitch is never surprised by a wrong fact. That is the build: enrichment + grounded owner stories + a rerunnable system that does not need to be rebuilt every two months [seller_pack]. The name-plus-LinkedIn Google-search method hits roughly 85% of owners where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. LinkedIn outreach on verified profiles runs around 40% reply rate versus the ~2% cold email baseline [seller_pack] — the same effort, twenty times the conversation rate. Pricing anchors at a flat base the team can afford: a comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission [seller_pack], a fraction of the $15k vendor quote for 40 records [vendor_refusal].

Opening line, in the seller's voice: "You've got a raw county pull and no pipeline right now — I can turn that into a ranked, story-grounded list where every owner claim traces to a real record, so the first city you expand to doesn't start with a 30%-wrong email list."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team stops being bottlenecked by geography. The county data they already hold becomes the seed for a rerunnable enrichment system — one that can be reloaded for the next city pull without rebuilding from scratch. The 30%-wrong contact problem gets cut down to a traceable, correctable layer rather than a silent tax on every outreach sequence. And the decision of whether to hire an assistant or expand the team gets made from a position of pipeline visibility, not pipeline drought — because the system surfaces who is actually distressed, ranked, before anyone gets in a car.

The county data they already hold becomes the seed for a rerunnable enrichment system — one that can be reloaded for the next city pull without rebuilding from scratch. The 30%-wrong contact problem gets cut down to a traceable, correctable layer rather than a silent tax on every outreach sequence. And the decision of whether to hire an assistant or expand the team gets made from a position of pipeline visibility, not pipeline drought — because the system surfaces who is actually distressed, ranked, before anyone gets in a car.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close 14 deals in LA was the proof-of-concept — now the team wants to replicate that machine in new cities. "The goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent]. To do that without burning the founder out, they need a pipeline that doesn't depend on someone physically driving streets or manually cross-referencing county CSVs.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now — "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. And the raw input they do have — a ~10k-row county CSV — is poisoned: "our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. You can't build a city-expansion playbook on a foundation where nearly one in three contact records is a dead end. Every wrong address or bad email is a door knocked on the wrong house, a mailer that bounced, a conversation that never happened.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Driving for dollars and manual BeenVerified-style lookups worked when the team was learning one market [manual_research]. But that approach doesn't scale to a second city — it scales to a second hire, then a third, then a manager to manage the hires. They already see it: "either we need to hire someone else on the team or I could get like an assistant" [expansion_intent]. The $15k vendor quote they walked away from — "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal] — confirms they know the manual path has a ceiling. Paying $15k for 40 enriched records at 30%-wrong data quality is not a system; it's an expensive guess.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the patty — takes the raw 10k-row county CSV and turns it into a grounded story for each record: who actually owns it, what their situation is, and which ones carry a real forced-sale signal (tax delinquency, absentee mailing address, distress proxy from build year + no remodel). Every claim traced to a real record, not a scraper guess. The name + LinkedIn Google-search hack hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong problem [data_pain]. The output is a durable CRM-style data system — rerunnable as the team enters each new city — not a one-time enrichment drop that needs to be rebuilt in two months. Pricing is structured the same way it worked for another real-estate investor client: flat base + commission, "anywhere from 4-5K would be sick" [seller_pack], which is a fraction of the $15k quote they already refused [vendor_refusal] and gets them a living system instead of 40 enriched rows.

Opening line (seller's voice): "You've got 10,000 rows sitting there and a pipeline at zero — I can tell you which 200 of those are actually in a forced-sale window before you spend a dollar on outreach or a day driving a new city."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team enters city two with a pre-sorted, story-grounded lead list — not a raw CSV and a prayer. The 30%-wrong contact rate drops because every record is verified before the outreach dollar is spent [data_pain / seller_pack]. The founder isn't hiring an assistant to do manual lookups; the system runs the lookups. And because the data layer is rerunnable [seller_pack], city three costs less to spin up than city two — the machine compounds instead of requiring a new hire at every new market. They stay in the powerful position: choosing which deals to pursue, not scrambling to find any deal at all.

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Scale the off-market acquisitions machine beyond LA — find distressed owners faster, in more cities, without adding headcount. In the founder's own framing: "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. "We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The team has a raw county data CSV but no CRM, no enrichment layer, and no reliable way to know which rows are worth calling. Their own email data is "consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain] — meaning roughly one in three outreach attempts misfires before it even starts.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Driving for dollars and manual title lookups worked at LA scale, but they hit the ceiling: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" [manual_research]. When the team looked at buying their way out of this with a vendor, the quote came back at "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched" — so they walked: "this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. Manual stays broken; the $15k vendor doesn't fix the 30%-wrong problem and doesn't scale to new cities. The only option they've named is hiring an assistant [expansion_intent] — but a hire doesn't rebuild the data layer, it just adds another person doing the same broken lookups.

THE BETTER PATH:
This is exactly the problem a durable enrichment system solves — not a one-time scrape that goes stale, but a rerunnable, CRM-style data layer that turns a raw county CSV into grounded stories: which owner is likely distressed, why, and what signal says so [seller_pack]. The 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method clears ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. LinkedIn outreach on clean, enriched data runs ~40% reply rate vs. ~2% on cold email [seller_pack] — that's the difference between a pipeline and a dead list. Pricing is structured as flat base + commission, anchored well below the $15k vendor the team already refused [seller_pack, vendor_refusal].

Opening line, in the seller's voice: "You're sitting on a county CSV that's probably 30% wrong — before you hire anyone or touch another city, let's find out which rows actually have a forced-sale story behind them."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team goes into the next city with a pre-sorted, story-backed lead list — not a spreadsheet full of bad emails and cold guesses. The 30%-wrong data problem gets cut before it burns outreach budget. The founder can evaluate new markets from a desk instead of a windshield, and the system reruns as the CSV grows — no rebuild every two months, no $15k vendor, no new hire just to do manual lookups in a different zip code. That's the position to plan from.

THE GOAL:
This team's mandate is simple and ambitious at once: stop working just LA and start replicating the off-market acquisition playbook in new cities. As the founder put it, "the goal is to start moving to other cities" [expansion_intent]. That means building a system that scales — not one that requires a person in a car.

THE OBSTACLE:
Right now there is no pipeline. "We finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap]. The team just completed a major cycle and the cupboard is bare. To refill it, they fall back on what they know: "The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving" [manual_research]. Driving works in one zip code. It does not work in Phoenix or Dallas. And even when the manual research produces a name, the contact data is broken — "our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. A 30% error rate on cold outreach means roughly one in three conversations never starts.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The team already looked for a shortcut and got burned by the price. A vendor quoted "fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. That refusal was rational — $15k for 40 records is not a system, it's a line item. But "stick doing it manually" is not a city-expansion strategy either. Manual driving-for-dollars does not port to a new market. A raw county data file sitting on a laptop, with no enrichment layer and no way to sort who is actually distressed versus who just has an old address, produces the same 30%-wrong dead ends at scale. The old way is not failing slowly — it already produced an empty pipeline at the exact moment the team wants to grow.

THE BETTER PATH:
What this team needs is not a vendor and not a hire — it is a durable data layer that turns a raw county pull into a ranked, story-grounded list of who is actually likely to sell and why, with every claim traced to a real record so the human making the pitch is never surprised by a wrong fact. That is the build: enrichment + grounded owner stories + a rerunnable system that does not need to be rebuilt every two months [seller_pack]. The name-plus-LinkedIn Google-search method hits roughly 85% of owners where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. LinkedIn outreach on verified profiles runs around 40% reply rate versus the ~2% cold email baseline [seller_pack] — the same effort, twenty times the conversation rate. Pricing anchors at a flat base the team can afford: a comparable real-estate investor engagement landed at $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission [seller_pack], a fraction of the $15k vendor quote for 40 records [vendor_refusal].

Opening line, in the seller's voice: "You've got a raw county pull and no pipeline right now — I can turn that into a ranked, story-grounded list where every owner claim traces to a real record, so the first city you expand to doesn't start with a 30%-wrong email list."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
The team stops being bottlenecked by geography. The county data they already hold becomes the seed for a rerunnable enrichment system — one that can be reloaded for the next city pull without rebuilding from scratch. The 30%-wrong contact problem gets cut down to a traceable, correctable layer rather than a silent tax on every outreach sequence. And the decision of whether to hire an assistant or expand the team gets made from a position of pipeline visibility, not pipeline drought — because the system surfaces who is actually distressed, ranked, before anyone gets in a car.
